text: The surgeon is performing a dissection at the hiatus during a parasophageal hiatal hernia repair, focusing on the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon dissects anteriorly on the right crus of the diaphragm, carefully maneuvering around a large vessel crossing through the pars flaccida, which limits visualization of the esophagus.
The surgeon clamps a vessel and injects three milligrams of indocyanine green (ICG) dye. The ICG fluorescence angiography shows the dye filling the lungs and then the peripheral tissues.

The surgeon observes that the left liver is not lighting up under fluorescence, indicating a lack of perfusion, while the CAUT-VIII and right liver appear bright green. The vessel is unclamped to restore perfusion.

The surgeon frees tissue in the pars flaccida to gain length on a vessel. After releasing the tissue, the left lobe of the liver lights up green, indicating successful perfusion and the need to preserve the vessel for the remainder of the dissection.
